description: capital of and state in Austria
description_wiki: Vienna (  vee-EN-ə; German: Wien [viːn] ; Austro-Bavarian: Wean [veɐ̯n]) is the capital, most populous city, and one of the nine states of Austria.

description: school of economic thought that advocates strict adherence to methodological individualism rather than focusing on aggregate variables, equilibrium analysis and societal groups
Austrian-American economist (1881–1973)
description_wiki: Ludwig Heinrich Edler von Mises (; German: [ˈluːtvɪç fɔn ˈmiːzəs]; September 29, 1881 – October 10, 1973) was an Austrian and American political economist and philosopher of the Austrian school.

description: public university in Vienna, Austria
description_wiki: The University of Vienna (German: Universität Wien, Austrian German: [univɛrsiˈtɛːt ˈviːn]) is a public research university in Vienna, Austria.

description: Austrian physicist (1838–1916)
description_wiki: Ernst Waldfried Josef Wenzel Mach ( MAHK; Austrian German: [ˈɛrnst ˈmax] ; 18 February 1838 – 19 February 1916) was an Austrian physicist and philosopher who contributed to the understanding of the physics of shock waves.

description: Viennese seminar of science and ideas active between 1921 and 1938

aliases: London School of Economics & Political Science
description: public university in London, United Kingdom
description_wiki: The London School of Economics and Political Science, commonly known as the London School of Economics or LSE, is a public research university in London, England, and a member institution of the University of London.

aliases: Lionel Charles Robbins, Baron Robbins of Clare Market
description: British economist (1898–1984)
group of macroeconomic theories
description_wiki: Keynesian economics ( KAYN-zee-ən; sometimes Keynesianism, named after British economist John Maynard Keynes) are the various macroeconomic theories and models of how aggregate demand (total spending in the economy) strongly influences economic output and inflation.

description: non-fiction work by Friedrich von Hayek
description_wiki: The Road to Serfdom is a book by the Austrian-British economist and philosopher Friedrich Hayek.

description: one of several PhD-granting committees at the University of Chicago
description_wiki: The John U. Nef Committee on Social Thought is a PhD-granting committee at the Social Sciences Division at the University of Chicago.

description: private university in Chicago, Illinois
description_wiki: The University of Chicago (UChicago, Chicago, UChi, or U of C) is a private research university in the Hyde Park community area of Chicago, Illinois, United States.

description: American economist (1885-1972)
Canadian economist (1892–1970)
description_wiki: Jacob Viner (3 May 1892 – 12 September 1970) was a Canadian economist and is considered with Frank Knight and Henry Simons to be one of the "inspiring" mentors of the early Chicago school of economics in the 1930s: he was one of the leading figures of the Chicago faculty.

aliases: Constitution of the United States of America
description: supreme law of the United States of America since March 4th, 1789

surface_forms: World War I was a historical watershed
description: global war originating in Europe, 1914–1918
description_wiki: World War I, or the First World War (28 July 1914 – 11 November 1918), also known as The Great War, was a global conflict between two coalitions: the Allies (or Entente) and the Central Powers.

description: school of economic thought that advocates strict adherence to methodological individualism rather than focusing on aggregate variables, equilibrium analysis and societal groups
Austrian philosopher and logician (1889–1951)
description_wiki: Ludwig Josef Johann Wittgenstein ( VIT-gən-s(h)tyne; Austrian German: [ˈluːtvɪç ˈjoːsɛf ˈjoːhan ˈvɪtɡn̩ʃtaɪn]; 26 April 1889 – 29 April 1951) was an Austro-British philosopher who worked primarily in logic, philosophy of mathematics, philosophy of mind, and philosophy of language.

description: legal system of ancient Rome and later the Roman and Byzantine Empire
description_wiki:
Roman law is the legal system of ancient Rome, including the legal developments spanning over a thousand years of jurisprudence, from the Twelve Tables (c. 449 BC), to the Corpus Juris Civilis (AD 529) ordered by Eastern Roman emperor Justinian I.
Roman law also denoted the legal system applied in most of Western Europe until the end of the 18th century.

description: Scottish moral philosopher and political economist (1723–1790)
description_wiki: Adam Smith (baptised 16 June  [O.S. 5 June] 1723 – 17 July 1790) was a Scottish economist and philosopher who was a pioneer in the field of political economy and key figure during the Scottish Enlightenment.

description: critique of central economic planning proposed by Ludwig von Mises
description_wiki: The economic calculation problem (ECP) is a criticism of using central economic planning (rather than market-based mechanisms) for the allocation of the factors of production.

description: Wikimedia disambiguation page

aliases: the socialist movement in the United Kingdom history
socialist movement in the United Kingdom history
description: aspect of history
description_wiki: Socialism in the United Kingdom is thought to stretch back to the 19th century, with roots arising in the English Civil War.

description: psychological theory that was founded in 1890 by the Viennese neurologist Sigmund Freud
description_wiki: Psychoanalysis is a set of theories and techniques to discover unconscious processes and their influence on conscious thought, emotion and behavior.
